# ClipForge

**AI Video Generation Platform for Marketers**

*Product Requirements Document (PRD)*

Version 1.0 \| Draft for Review

Prepared: July 2, 2026

Single-tenant deployment \| Not for external distribution

## Document Control

### Revision History

| **Version** | **Date**   | **Author**                 | **Summary of Changes**                                                     |
|-------------|------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1         | 2026-07-02 | Product (draft via Claude) | Initial PRD compiled from stakeholder requirements and AI model research   |
| 1.0         | 2026-07-02 | Product                    | First complete draft covering scope, architecture, data model, and roadmap |

1. Executive Summary

2. Product Name & Positioning

3. Goals & Success Metrics

4. Target Users & Personas

5. Scope

6. Core Feature Requirements

7. Additional Recommended Features

8. AI Video Model Integration

10. Data Model

11. API Design

12. Credit / Rate-Limit Handling

13. Video Merge & Export

15. Non-Functional Requirements

16. Security, Privacy & Compliance

18. Roadmap & Milestones

19. Risks & Mitigations

20. Open Questions & Assumptions

## 1. Executive Summary

ClipForge is a single-tenant web platform that lets marketers turn plain-language instructions into finished promotional videos without touching a traditional video editor. A user describes an overall visual style, breaks their idea into scenes, and generates an 8-second AI video clip per scene using one of three selectable models: Google Gemini Veo Pro, Google Gemini Veo Flash, or MiniMax Hailuo 2.3. Once satisfied with the individual clips, the user selects the ones they want, arranges them in order, and merges them into a single downloadable video file.

The platform is built on a standard, open-source web stack (React, Express, PostgreSQL, Redis, BullMQ) so that it is inexpensive to run, easy to hire for, and simple to self-host. Because third-party video generation is slow (tens of seconds to a few minutes per clip) and metered per second of output, the system is architected around an asynchronous job queue, per-user/per-model daily quotas, and clear in-UI feedback so that non-technical marketers never have to wonder what the system is doing or why a generation was blocked.

This document defines the product scope, user experience, system architecture, data model, third-party model integration details, and phased roadmap required to build ClipForge.

## 2. Product Name & Positioning

Recommended Name: ClipForge

“ClipForge” is short, easy to say over a call, easy to spell, and immediately communicates the core action of the product — forging finished videos out of individual AI-generated clips. It reads well as a URL (clipforge.app / clipforge.io), a wordmark, and a Slack/Notion mention (“we made this in ClipForge”).

Alternate name candidates considered

| **Name**   | **Rationale**                                       | **Concern**                                              |
|------------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| Scenely    | Friendly, emphasizes “scenes” building block        | Slightly generic, sounds like a photo app                |
| Reelcraft  | Speaks to marketers who think in “reels”            | Close to existing “Craft” branded products               |
| Scenestack | Literal description of stacking scenes into a video | Two-word compound is harder to say aloud                 |
| Adreel     | Signals the marketing/ad use case directly          | Narrows the product to ads only, less durable as a brand |

Recommendation: proceed with ClipForge as the working product name for this PRD and all technical artifacts (repo name, DB name, env vars use clipforge as the slug).

## 3. Goals & Success Metrics

### 3.1 Product Goals

- Let a non-technical marketer go from an idea to a downloadable multi-scene video in under 15 minutes of active effort.

- Support 3 interchangeable AI video models so cost and quality can be balanced per project.

- Make generation cost and quota state visible and understandable at all times — no silent failures or surprise blocks.

- Ship on a boring, well-documented open-source stack that a small team can operate and debug.

### 3.2 Success Metrics (initial targets, to be refined post-launch)

| **Metric**                                                         | **Target (first 90 days)**         |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Time from project creation to first generated clip                 | \< 3 minutes median                |
| Clip generation success rate (non-content-policy failures)         | \> 95%                             |
| % of projects that reach a completed merged export                 | \> 40%                             |
| Regeneration rate per scene (quality signal, track not force down) | Baseline only, monitor trend       |
| Daily MiniMax quota exhaustion rate                                | \< 20% of active users hit the cap |

## 4. Target Users & Personas

ClipForge is built for marketers and small creative/growth teams producing short promotional video content — not for professional video editors or VFX artists. The UI must therefore favor guided, opinionated workflows over raw technical controls.

Persona A — “Solo Growth Marketer”

- Runs paid social and organic content for a startup or SMB, often solo.

- Comfortable writing marketing copy and prompts, not comfortable with editing software.

- Wants to produce 3–6 short ad variants per week for A/B testing on social channels.

Persona B — “In-house Content Lead”

- Manages a content calendar across product marketing and social.

- Cares about brand consistency (global style) across every scene of every video.

- Needs to regenerate scenes quickly when stakeholders give feedback.

Persona C — “Agency Producer”

- Produces videos for multiple client products/services (still single-tenant per deployment).

- Cost-conscious — wants to know which model to use for drafts vs. final delivery.

- Needs a reliable way to assemble scene clips into one deliverable file to send to a client.

## 5. Scope

### 5.1 In Scope (V1)

- Single-tenant deployment; one internal user base (no organizations/workspaces isolation logic).

- Email/password authentication with session-based login to a dashboard.

- Project creation with a global style prompt and an ordered list of scenes (2..N scenes).

- Per-scene text-to-video generation via Veo Pro, Veo Flash, or MiniMax Hailuo 2.3 (max 8s per clip).

- Per-model, per-user daily generation quotas with clear UI feedback (MiniMax: 3/day by default, configurable).

- In-browser video preview per scene/clip, with a full generation history per scene.

- Regeneration of any scene with an edited prompt and/or a different model, without losing prior attempts.

- Selection of one clip per scene (or omit a scene) and ordered merge into a single output video.

- Download of the merged video file.

### 5.2 Out of Scope (V1)

- Multi-tenant org/workspace management, billing, or seat-based permissions.

- Timeline-based non-linear video editing (trimming mid-clip, transitions, text overlays) — candidate for V2.

- Native mobile apps (responsive web only).

- Real-time multi-user collaborative editing of the same project.

- Custom fine-tuned / brand-trained video models.

## 6. Core Feature Requirements

### 6.1 Authentication & Dashboard

- Users log in with email + password (bcrypt/argon2 hashed, server-side sessions or JWT + refresh token).

- Dashboard lists the user’s projects as cards: title, thumbnail (first completed clip), scene count, status (draft / generating / ready to merge / exported), last updated.

- “New Project” CTA is the primary action on the dashboard.

- Basic account settings page: change password, view daily quota usage per model.

### 6.2 Project & Scene Creation

Creating a project is a guided, form-based flow, not a blank canvas:

1.  Step 1 — Project title + Global Style instructions (free text; e.g. “bright, energetic product ad, handheld camera, warm color grade”). This text is prepended/merged into every scene prompt sent to the model so tone stays consistent.

2.  Step 2 — Add Scene 1’s text instructions. A “+ Add Scene” button appends Scene 2, 3…N. Scenes are reorderable via drag-and-drop (using a library such as dnd-kit).

3.  Step 3 — Per scene, choose the default model (can be changed per-generation later) and generate.

Each scene supports:

- A live character counter and prompt-writing tips (“describe subject, action, camera movement, and mood”), since these users are not prompt experts.

- An optional reference/first-frame image upload for models that support image-to-video (Veo and Hailuo 2.3 both support this — see Section 8).

- A visible per-scene status chip: Not generated / Queued / Generating / Ready / Failed.

### 6.3 Model Selection

Model choice is exposed as a simple, visual picker (radio-card group) at the scene level, defaulting to the project’s last-used model. Each card shows, at a glance:

- Model name and a one-line description (“Highest quality, cinematic” / “Fast & affordable drafts” / “Strong character consistency”).

- Relative cost indicator (\$ / \$\$ / \$\$\$).

- Remaining daily quota for that model, if the model is quota-limited (e.g. “2 of 3 MiniMax generations left today”).

- A disabled state with tooltip when the daily quota is exhausted, rather than allowing the click and failing server-side.

### 6.4 Generation Lifecycle

Generation is always asynchronous:

4.  User clicks Generate on a scene → API validates quota, enqueues a BullMQ job, returns a generation record with status “queued” immediately.

5.  UI shows a non-blocking progress indicator on that scene’s card; the user can keep editing other scenes while this runs.

6.  A worker calls the selected provider’s API, polls or receives a webhook for completion, downloads the finished asset, and uploads it to object storage.

7.  Status updates are pushed to the browser via WebSocket/SSE (with polling fallback) so the UI updates without a manual refresh.

8.  On success, the clip becomes playable inline immediately. On failure, a human-readable error and a Retry action are shown (see Section 12 for quota/error taxonomy).

### 6.5 Regeneration & History

- Every generation attempt for a scene is stored, not overwritten — users can compare multiple takes side by side.

- Regenerate button opens the scene’s prompt pre-filled with the last-used text so the user can tweak rather than retype.

- User can switch models between attempts (e.g. draft on Veo Flash, finalize on Veo Pro).

- User explicitly marks one generation per scene as “selected” — this is the version used for preview and for the merge step. Selecting a new take doesn’t delete older takes.

### 6.6 Preview

- Every generated clip is playable directly in the browser via an HTML5 \<video\> element (no download required to review).

- A project-level “Storyboard Preview” plays all currently-selected scene clips back-to-back client-side (sequential playback, not yet a real merged file) so the user gets a feel for pacing before spending a merge job.

### 6.7 Select, Order & Merge

Once one or more scenes have a ready clip:

- User enters a “Merge” view listing every scene with its selected clip thumbnail and a checkbox (default: checked for all scenes with a ready clip).

- Scenes can be reordered in this view independent of their creation order (drag-and-drop), so the final video order does not have to match how scenes were authored.

- Pressing “Merge Selected” enqueues a merge job (FFmpeg concat, transcoded to a consistent resolution/frame rate/codec since the three providers may return different specs).

- On completion, the merged video is playable in-browser and downloadable as an MP4 via a signed URL.

- Past merges are kept in the project’s Export History so a user can re-download or compare previous cuts.

## 7. Additional Recommended Features

Based on the current capabilities of Veo 3.1 and MiniMax Hailuo 2.3 (researched July 2026), the following features are recommended in addition to the core requirements. They are grouped by priority.

### 7.1 Recommended for V1 (low effort, high value)

- Image-to-Video / first-frame reference: both Veo and Hailuo 2.3 accept a starting image. Letting a marketer upload a product photo as the first frame dramatically improves brand/product accuracy versus text-only prompts.

- Prompt Optimizer toggle: MiniMax’s API exposes a “prompt_optimizer” flag that rewrites terse prompts into higher-quality ones — valuable for non-expert users and should default to on.

- Aspect ratio / resolution presets: expose simple presets (“Square – Instagram feed”, “Vertical – Reels/TikTok”, “Widescreen – YouTube/web”) rather than raw pixel dimensions.

- Cost/credit estimator shown before generating: “This will use 1 of your 3 daily MiniMax generations” or the estimated \$ cost for Veo, so users aren’t surprised.

- Content-safety handling: all three providers run their own safety filters and can silently reject a prompt. Surface a clear, friendly “this prompt was flagged, please rephrase” message with the provider’s reason where available, and do not deduct quota for a failed/rejected generation (mirrors MiniMax’s own policy of not charging failed generations).

- Watermark toggle: Hailuo’s API exposes an aigc_watermark parameter; default to on for compliance with AI-content-labeling norms, with an admin-level override.

- Basic brand kit: save a default global style + a default reference/logo image at the account level to speed up new project creation.

### 7.2 Recommended for V2

- Native audio awareness: Veo 3.1 can generate synchronized native audio (dialogue, SFX, ambience). Add an “include audio” toggle per scene once budget allows, since it changes per-second pricing.

- Background music & simple audio mixing on the merged export (e.g. via FFmpeg audio track overlay + a small royalty-free music library).

- Auto-captioning/subtitles burned into the merged export for silent-autoplay social placements.

- Scene transition options for the merge step (hard cut, cross-fade, fade-to-black) via FFmpeg xfade filters.

- Character/subject consistency helper: reuse a generated frame or reference image across scenes automatically to keep the same “actor” or product consistent, leaning on Hailuo’s subject-reference mode and Veo’s image conditioning.

- Team comments/approval flow (still single-tenant, but multiple logins with lightweight roles: Editor / Viewer) if the platform later needs internal review cycles.

- Usage analytics dashboard: generations per model, spend estimate, success/failure rate, most-regenerated scenes.

### 7.3 Explicitly Deferred

- Timeline video editor (multi-track trimming, keyframe text overlays) — large scope, contradicts the “not video experts” design goal; revisit only if user research shows strong demand.

- Multi-tenant billing/workspaces — explicitly out of scope per current requirements.

- Fine-tuned/custom model training — not offered by any of the three providers today in a way that fits a lightweight marketer tool.

## 8. AI Video Model Integration

The product treats all three models through a single internal “VideoProvider” interface (generate, checkStatus, cancel) so new models can be added later without UI rework. Provider-specific quirks are normalized at the integration layer. All figures below reflect public pricing/spec pages as of July 2026 and should be re-verified against each provider’s live docs before launch, since AI video pricing changes frequently.

### 8.1 Model Comparison

|                        | **Gemini Veo 3.1 (Pro/“Quality”)**              | **Gemini Veo 3.1 Flash/Fast**       | **MiniMax Hailuo 2.3**                                                         |
|------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Best for               | Highest-fidelity hero shots, final deliverables | Fast, cheaper drafts and iteration  | Expressive characters, strong prompt adherence, image-to-video                 |
| Native max clip length | 8 seconds per generation                        | 8 seconds per generation            | 6 or 10 seconds (product will cap at 8s for UI consistency)                    |
| Resolution options     | 720p / 1080p, 4K at a premium                   | 720p / 1080p                        | 768p or 1080p (1080p limited to 6s)                                            |
| Native audio           | Yes, optional, synchronized                     | Yes, optional                       | Not a core feature; treat as silent clips                                      |
| Image-to-video         | Yes                                             | Yes                                 | Yes, plus a dedicated Subject-Reference mode for character/product consistency |
| Indicative API cost    | ~\$0.20–\$0.40 per second                       | ~\$0.10–\$0.15 per second           | Point-based; roughly \$0.025–\$0.05 per second depending on plan               |
| Quota strategy in V1   | Soft daily cap, cost-based alerting             | Soft daily cap, cost-based alerting | Hard daily cap of 3 generations per user (configurable)                        |

Note: Google has been actively deprecating older Veo endpoints (Veo 2 and initial Veo 3.0 generate endpoints were slated for shutdown mid-2026) in favor of Veo 3.1 GA endpoints. The integration layer must pin exact model IDs in configuration (not hard-coded in application logic) so the provider version can be bumped without a code deploy.

### 8.2 Clip Length Policy

Per the product requirement, every model is capped at 8 seconds maximum per generated clip in the ClipForge UI — this matches Veo’s hard technical maximum and is enforced as a product-level ceiling for MiniMax (which technically supports 10s at 768p) to keep pricing, UX copy, and merge-timing predictable across all three models. If a user needs a longer moment, the recommended pattern is to generate two consecutive scenes and let the merge step stitch them together.

### 8.3 Integration Pattern

- All three providers are asynchronous/job-based (submit → poll or webhook → retrieve asset), so the BullMQ worker pattern in Section 9 applies uniformly.

- Workers should implement exponential backoff polling (e.g. every 5–10s) with a max wait ceiling (~5 minutes) after which the job is marked failed and eligible for manual retry.

- Generated assets are downloaded from the provider and re-hosted on the platform’s own object storage — never linked directly — so playback isn’t dependent on the provider’s URL lifetime and so usage can be audited.

- API keys for each provider are stored server-side only (never shipped to the browser) and read from a secrets manager / environment variables.

## 9. System Architecture

ClipForge follows a classic web app + async worker architecture. The browser never talks to AI providers directly; all provider calls happen server-side inside queue workers, which keeps API keys secret and makes quota enforcement and cost tracking reliable.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Figure 1 - High-level system architecture" style="max-width:100%;" />

*Figure 1 — High-level system architecture*

### 9.1 Component Responsibilities

| **Component**                                                                    | **Responsibility**                                                                                                                                                                            |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| React SPA                                                                        | Auth screens, dashboard, project/scene builder, model picker, preview, merge UI. Talks only to the Express API.                                                                               |
| Express.js API                                                                   | REST endpoints for auth/projects/scenes/generations/merges; validates input; enforces quota checks against Redis/PostgreSQL before enqueueing jobs; issues signed URLs for playback/download. |
| WebSocket/SSE layer                                                              | Pushes generation and merge job status to the browser in real time; falls back to polling if the connection drops.                                                                            |
| BullMQ generation queue + workers                                                | Calls the selected provider’s API, polls for completion, downloads the result, writes it to object storage, updates PostgreSQL.                                                               |
| BullMQ merge queue + worker                                                      | Downloads selected clips, normalizes them (resolution/fps/codec) with FFmpeg, concatenates in the chosen order, uploads the merged file.                                                      |
| PostgreSQL                                                                       | System of record: users, projects, scenes, generations, usage counters, merged videos.                                                                                                        |
| Redis                                                                            | BullMQ job backing store, session/rate-limit counters, short-lived caches.                                                                                                                    |
| Object Storage (S3-compatible: AWS S3, Cloudflare R2, or MinIO for self-hosting) | Stores raw generated clips and merged output files.                                                                                                                                           |
| CDN                                                                              | Serves video assets to the browser via signed, time-limited URLs.                                                                                                                             |

## 10. Data Model

The schema below captures the core entities. A relational database (PostgreSQL) is used because relationships between projects, scenes, generations, and merges are strongly structured and benefit from transactional integrity (e.g., decrementing a quota counter and creating a generation record must be atomic).

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Figure 2 - Core entity-relationship model" style="max-width:100%;" />

*Figure 2 — Core entity-relationship model*

### 10.1 Key Entities

- users — account credentials and profile.

- projects — one video project; holds the global style prompt.

- scenes — ordered building blocks of a project; each has its own prompt and points at a “selected” generation.

- generations — every individual model call/attempt for a scene, including failed ones, model used, cost, and resulting asset URL.

- usage_counters — per-user, per-model, per-day counters used to enforce quotas (e.g., MiniMax = 3/day).

- merged_videos — a finished export: an ordered list of chosen generation IDs plus the resulting output file.

### 10.2 Notes

- generations.status uses an enum: queued, running, succeeded, failed, content_flagged.

- Soft-deletes (deleted_at) are recommended on projects/scenes so accidental deletion is recoverable within a grace window.

- All monetary/quota figures are stored as integers in the smallest unit (cost_units) to avoid floating-point drift; a display-layer formatter converts to \$ or “x of y remaining”.

## 11. API Design

REST over JSON, versioned under /api/v1. Authenticated endpoints require a Bearer session token. Generation and merge endpoints are fire-and-forget (return 202 Accepted with a job/resource id); clients subscribe to status via WebSocket or poll GET endpoints.

| **Method & Path**                   | **Purpose**                                                                            |
|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| POST /api/v1/auth/login             | Authenticate, return session token                                                     |
| POST /api/v1/auth/logout            | Invalidate session                                                                     |
| GET /api/v1/projects                | List current user’s projects                                                           |
| POST /api/v1/projects               | Create a project (title + global style)                                                |
| GET /api/v1/projects/:id            | Get project with scenes + generation history                                           |
| PATCH /api/v1/projects/:id          | Update title/global style                                                              |
| POST /api/v1/projects/:id/scenes    | Add a scene (prompt, order_index)                                                      |
| PATCH /api/v1/scenes/:id            | Edit prompt / reorder / set selected generation                                        |
| DELETE /api/v1/scenes/:id           | Remove a scene                                                                         |
| POST /api/v1/scenes/:id/generations | Enqueue a generation (model, prompt override, optional reference image) — 202 Accepted |
| GET /api/v1/scenes/:id/generations  | List generation history/status for a scene                                             |
| GET /api/v1/generations/:id         | Poll a single generation’s status/result (fallback to WS)                              |
| GET /api/v1/usage                   | Current user’s daily quota usage per model                                             |
| POST /api/v1/projects/:id/merge     | Enqueue a merge job with ordered generation ids — 202 Accepted                         |
| GET /api/v1/merges/:id              | Poll merge job status / get signed download URL                                        |
| WS /ws/jobs                         | Real-time status push for generation & merge jobs owned by the connected user          |

## 12. Credit / Rate-Limit Handling

Because MiniMax Hailuo 2.3 is capped at 3 generations/day per the product requirement (and because Veo usage should also be cost-monitored even without a hard block), quota logic is centralized in a single QuotaService used by the API before any job is enqueued.

### 12.1 Enforcement Flow

9.  User requests a generation for model X.

10. API reads (and, inside a DB transaction, locks) the user’s usage_counters row for (user_id, model, today’s date).

11. If count_used \>= daily_limit, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests with a machine-readable code (quota_exceeded) and the reset time (midnight UTC or account timezone, configurable).

12. If under the limit, the API increments count_used, creates the generation record as queued, and enqueues the BullMQ job — the increment and the job creation happen in the same transaction so a crash can’t enqueue an uncounted job.

13. If the job ultimately fails or is flagged by the provider’s content safety system, the API refunds (decrements) count_used, mirroring MiniMax’s own policy of not charging for failed generations.

### 12.2 UI Behavior

- Model picker cards show live remaining quota and disable the model with an explanatory tooltip once exhausted, rather than surfacing a generic API error after the click.

- A persistent, dismissible banner appears when a user is at 1 remaining generation for a limited model (“Heads up: you have 1 MiniMax generation left today”).

- Errors distinguish clearly between: quota exceeded (wait until reset), content policy rejection (edit your prompt), and transient provider failure (auto-retry available).

### 12.3 Defaults (configurable via admin settings, not hard-coded)

| **Model**          | **Default daily limit**                        |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Gemini Veo Pro     | Soft cap — alert only, e.g. 10/day, cost-based |
| Gemini Veo Flash   | Soft cap — alert only, e.g. 20/day, cost-based |
| MiniMax Hailuo 2.3 | Hard cap — 3/day per user                      |

## 13. Video Merge & Export

### 13.1 Selection & Ordering UX

- The Merge screen shows every scene that has at least one ready clip, pre-checked, in scene order.

- Users can uncheck scenes to exclude them and drag to reorder — the final sequence is independent of authoring order.

- A live estimated total duration updates as scenes are checked/unchecked (sum of each selected clip’s length, max 8s each).

### 13.2 Processing

- The merge job runs FFmpeg server-side: normalizes each input clip to a common resolution, frame rate, and codec (H.264/AAC in an MP4 container is the safe default for broad compatibility), then concatenates via the concat demuxer/filter.

- V1 uses hard cuts between clips; V2 adds selectable transitions (cross-fade, fade-to-black) via FFmpeg’s xfade filter (see Section 7.2).

- Merge jobs run in their own BullMQ queue, separate from generation jobs, so a burst of merges doesn’t starve new-clip generation throughput (and vice versa).

### 13.3 Output & Download

- Completed merges are playable inline and downloadable via a signed, time-limited URL from object storage/CDN.

- Every merge is retained in an Export History list on the project so previous cuts remain accessible without re-running the job.

## 14. UX/UI Principles

The primary user is a marketer, not a video engineer, so the interface must optimize for confidence and clarity over configurability.

- Guided, linear flows (style → scenes → generate → review → merge) rather than an open canvas.

- Every long-running action (generation, merge) shows visible, non-blocking progress — never a silent spinner with no explanation and never a UI that appears frozen.

- Plain-language error and quota messaging; no raw provider error codes shown to end users.

- Sensible defaults everywhere (default model, default aspect ratio, prompt optimizer on) so a first-time user can succeed without reading documentation.

- Built with shadcn/ui components on top of Tailwind for a clean, accessible, consistent design system; TanStack Query manages all server-state/caching so loading and error states are handled consistently across the app; TanStack Table for any tabular views (e.g., usage history, export history); TanStack Router for type-safe client routing.

- Mobile-responsive layout for review/approval on the go, even though authoring is optimized for desktop.

## 15. Non-Functional Requirements

| **Category**    | **Requirement**                                                                                                                                       |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Performance     | API p95 response time \< 300ms for non-generation endpoints; generation job enqueue confirmed to client in \< 500ms.                                  |
| Scalability     | Workers scale horizontally and independently from the API (separate deployable process/container); queue depth is monitored and alertable.            |
| Availability    | Target 99.5% uptime for the web app and API; provider outages degrade gracefully (that model shown as temporarily unavailable, others remain usable). |
| Reliability     | Generation/merge jobs are idempotent and safely retryable; at-least-once job processing with dedupe keys.                                             |
| Observability   | Structured logging, job-level tracing (request id → job id → provider job id), and dashboards for queue depth, failure rate, and per-model spend.     |
| Accessibility   | WCAG 2.1 AA target for core flows (forms, buttons, video controls, color contrast).                                                                   |
| Browser support | Latest two versions of Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari; graceful video-codec fallback.                                                                  |
| Data retention  | Generated clips and merges retained indefinitely by default, configurable per deployment for storage cost control.                                    |

## 16. Security, Privacy & Compliance

- Passwords hashed with bcrypt or argon2; sessions/JWTs are short-lived with refresh rotation; all traffic over HTTPS/TLS.

- Third-party AI provider API keys are stored server-side (secrets manager or encrypted env vars) and are never exposed to the client bundle.

- Object storage assets are private by default; access is only via short-lived signed URLs, never public buckets.

- Rate limiting (Redis-backed) on auth endpoints to prevent brute force, in addition to the model-generation quota system.

- AI-generated content should be labeled per provider requirements (e.g., MiniMax’s aigc_watermark) to support transparency and platform policy compliance for downstream ad placements (e.g., Meta/Google ad policies increasingly require AI-content disclosure).

- Basic input sanitization/validation on all prompt text and file uploads (type/size limits on reference images).

- Audit log of generation and merge actions (who, when, what model, what cost) for internal cost accountability, even without multi-tenant billing.

## 17. Tech Stack Summary

| **Layer**                    | **Choice**                                                                                                    | **Notes**                                                                   |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Frontend framework           | React (latest stable) + Vite                                                                                  | SPA served as static assets                                                 |
| UI components                | shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS                                                                                      | Accessible, themeable component primitives                                  |
| Data fetching / server state | TanStack Query                                                                                                | Caching, retries, background refetch for job status polling fallback        |
| Routing                      | TanStack Router                                                                                               | Type-safe client-side routing                                               |
| Tables                       | TanStack Table                                                                                                | Usage history, export history, admin views                                  |
| Backend framework            | Express.js (latest stable)                                                                                    | REST API + WebSocket/SSE upgrade                                            |
| Database                     | PostgreSQL                                                                                                    | System of record; use Prisma or Drizzle ORM for type-safe schema/migrations |
| Cache / queue backing store  | Redis                                                                                                         | Session/rate-limit counters + BullMQ backing store                          |
| Job queue                    | BullMQ                                                                                                        | Separate queues for generation and merge; horizontally scalable workers     |
| Video processing             | FFmpeg (via fluent-ffmpeg or direct CLI invocation)                                                           | Normalization and concatenation for merge                                   |
| Object storage               | S3-compatible (AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2 / self-hosted MinIO)                                                    | Raw clips + merged exports                                                  |
| Auth                         | Custom email/password + session (or JWT) — open-source, no vendor lock-in                                     | bcrypt/argon2 for hashing                                                   |
| Deployment                   | Docker containers; API, workers, and frontend as separate services behind a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx/Caddy) | docker-compose for local/dev; same images promoted to staging/prod          |
| Monitoring                   | Open-source stack: e.g., Prometheus + Grafana for metrics, and a log aggregator (Loki/ELK)                    | Optional but recommended given async job criticality                        |

## 18. Roadmap & Milestones

Phase 0 — Foundations (Weeks 1–2)

- Repo scaffolding, CI, Docker Compose dev environment, auth, base DB schema and migrations.

Phase 1 — MVP Core Loop (Weeks 3–6)

- Project/scene creation UI, single-model generation (start with Veo Flash for speed/cost while integration is validated), async job pipeline end-to-end, in-browser preview.

Phase 2 — Multi-Model + Quotas (Weeks 7–8)

- Add Veo Pro and MiniMax Hailuo 2.3 providers behind the common interface, model picker UI, quota service and MiniMax daily cap enforcement.

Phase 3 — Regeneration, Select & Merge (Weeks 9–11)

- Generation history UI, regenerate flow, merge selection/ordering screen, FFmpeg merge worker, export history, download.

Phase 4 — Polish & V1 Launch (Weeks 12–13)

- Prompt optimizer toggle, image-to-video/reference upload, cost/quota UI polish, accessibility pass, observability dashboards, load testing of the queue pipeline.

Phase 5 — V2 Candidates (post-launch, prioritized by usage data)

- Native audio, background music, transitions, subtitles, brand kit, usage analytics dashboard (see Section 7.2).

## 19. Risks & Mitigations

| **Risk**                                                                                                                | **Mitigation**                                                                                                                                                    |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Third-party model pricing or endpoint versions change frequently (Google has deprecated Veo endpoints mid-cycle before) | Provider config (model IDs, pricing) lives in an admin-editable config table, not hard-coded; integration layer abstracts provider specifics behind one interface |
| Generation latency (tens of seconds to minutes) frustrates users expecting instant results                              | Fully async UX with clear progress states; allow users to keep working on other scenes while a job runs                                                           |
| Content-safety rejections from providers confuse non-expert users                                                       | Friendly, specific in-UI messaging; no quota charged on rejected/failed jobs; prompt-writing tips shown proactively                                               |
| Merge step fails due to mismatched clip resolution/codec/frame-rate across providers                                    | Always normalize every clip to a common target spec in the merge worker before concatenation                                                                      |
| Cost overrun from unmetered Veo usage                                                                                   | Soft caps with proactive cost alerts even where not hard-blocked; per-model spend visible in an internal usage dashboard                                          |
| Queue backlog during traffic spikes                                                                                     | Horizontally scalable workers, queue depth monitoring/alerting, and configurable concurrency per provider to respect their own rate limits                        |

## 20. Open Questions & Assumptions

Assumptions Made in This Draft

- “Single-tenant” means one shared user pool with individual logins, not full workspace/org isolation — confirm this matches intent.

- The 8-second cap applies uniformly across all three models as a product decision, even though MiniMax technically supports up to 10s at 768p.

- MiniMax’s 3/day limit is per user; Veo does not have a hard requirement stated, so it is treated as cost-monitored rather than hard-capped, pending confirmation.

Questions for Stakeholders

- Should the MiniMax daily limit (and any Veo soft caps) reset at midnight UTC, or per-user local time?

- Is native audio (Veo’s synchronized audio generation) desired for V1, or is silent-clip + separate music track acceptable initially?

- What is the expected concurrent user count at launch? This determines initial worker concurrency and Redis/Postgres sizing.

- Should merged exports support resolutions/aspect ratios beyond what the source clips were generated at, or should the export always match the scene generation settings?

- Is a self-hosted deployment (e.g., on-prem or private cloud) required, or is a managed cloud deployment (AWS/GCP) acceptable? This affects the object storage and secrets-management choice.
